summary: Graham argues that much of the next generation of software will move from desktop to server-based applications because the model is far more convenient for users and more efficient for developers. This shift changes how software is built, released, supported, and monetized, creating a landscape where small, fast startups can outperform large incumbents like Microsoft.
key_points: Server-based apps reduce user friction: no installs, upgrades, or data trapped on a single machine.
Web-based delivery enables rapid, incremental releases that cut bugs and improve developer feedback loops.
Operating software on servers reshapes engineering into a systems-and-services discipline, not just coding.
Subscription revenue and easier purchasing make web apps a stronger business model.
The shift favors small startups over big companies because speed and intensity matter more than scale.
money_quotes: The whole idea of "your computer" is going away, and being replaced with "your data."
With server-based software, you can make changes almost as you would in a program you were writing for yourself.
Desktop software forces users to become system administrators. Web-based software forces programmers to.
Web pages weren't designed to be a UI for applications, but they're just good enough.
You can literally launch your product as three guys sitting in the living room of an apartment, and a server collocated at an ISP.
key_insight: Server-based software flips the old desktop model by making apps easier for users and faster to iterate, which in turn gives small startups a structural advantage over large incumbents.
